county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1873; Reconstruction-era formation; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Lincoln Parish Clerk of Court (Ruston). Formed 1873 from Bienville, Claiborne, Jackson, and Union. Home to Louisiana Tech University and Grambling State University. Pre-1873 records split among parent parishes. Ruston area families. North Louisiana uplands. Grambling State University archives have African American history collections for the region. Lincoln Parish Library has genealogical materials. Protestant church records predominate. Named for Abraham Lincoln — unusual for a former Confederate state. Cross-reference with Bienville and Claiborne for earlier records.

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln Parish Conveyance Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln Parish Clerk of Court, Ruston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Conveyance (deed) books for Lincoln Parish from 1873 forward; Louisiana uses civil law system — property transfers recorded as notarial acts

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln Parish Succession Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln Parish Clerk of Court, Ruston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Succession records (Louisiana's civil law equivalent of probate) for Lincoln Parish; includes inventories, family meetings (conseil de famille), and partition records naming heirs

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln Parish Marriage Records — Clerk of Court / Catholic Church Registers
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln Parish Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil marriage licenses and Catholic church marriage registers for Lincoln Parish; church records often predate civil registration
source_profiles.survival_notes: Statewide vital records registration from 1914; earlier records at parish level and in church registers

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln Parish Church Records — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian
source_profiles.repository: Local congregations / Louisiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Protestant church records for Lincoln Parish including membership rolls, baptisms, marriages, and burials; supplements civil records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1832; antebellum parish; Florida Parishes (former Spanish West Florida)
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Livingston Parish Clerk of Court (Livingston). Formed 1832 from St. Helena. Florida Parishes region — part of Spanish West Florida, then Republic of West Florida (1810). Piney woods and farming heritage. Pre-1832 records in St. Helena. Livingston Parish Library has local history collections. Protestant churches (Baptist, Methodist) predominate in this Anglo-American settled region. Cross-reference with St. Helena and East Feliciana. Some Acadian and Italian (Tickfaw area) settlement. Named for Edward Livingston, US statesman who helped draft Louisiana's civil code.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1838; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Madison Parish Clerk of Court (Tallulah). Formed 1838 from Concordia. Mississippi River delta cotton plantation parish with one of the largest enslaved populations in Louisiana. Tallulah area families. Cross-reference with Concordia and Tensas. Major source for enslaved persons research — succession records list enslaved individuals by name. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA document post-war transitions. Madison Parish Library has local history. Directly across the river from Vicksburg, MS — cross-reference Mississippi records. Northeast Louisiana delta region.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1844; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Morehouse Parish Clerk of Court (Bastrop). Formed 1844 from Ouachita. Northeast Louisiana — cotton and later natural gas economy. Pre-1844 records in Ouachita. Bastrop area families. Morehouse Parish Library has local genealogical materials. Anglo-American settlement predominates. Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist) are primary religious sources. Mer Rouge area — site of notorious 1922 Ku Klux Klan murders. Cross-reference with Ouachita and West Carroll for related families. Boeuf River area plantation records for antebellum period.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Central Louisiana; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Natchitoches Parish Clerk of Court (Natchitoches). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Oldest permanent settlement in Louisiana (1714) — Fort St. Jean Baptiste. Cane River Creole community — Cane River Creole National Heritage Area has extensive records of free people of color, including the famous Metoyer family and Melrose Plantation. Northwestern State University Watson Memorial Library has Cane River collections. Catholic church registers at Immaculate Conception (Natchitoches) from 1729. French and Spanish colonial records extensive. Notarial archives from the 1730s. Parent parish for many North Louisiana parishes. Iron Horse Trail — Camino Real heritage.

county_metadata.record_losses: Various fires damaged portions of records; 1788 and 1794 fires destroyed much of colonial New Orleans; Hurricane Katrina (2005) damaged some records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; New Orleans — cultural and economic hub, primary research center for Louisiana genealogy; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Orleans Parish Civil District Court (New Orleans). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). CRITICAL RESEARCH PARISH: New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center has 30+ million pages of notarial acts from 1733 — unique in the US. Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives has sacramental records from 1718 including St. Louis Cathedral records. New Orleans Public Library Louisiana Division has extensive city records. Historic New Orleans Collection (THNOC) and Tulane University Louisiana Research Collection. Amistad Research Center (Tulane) for African American records. Free people of color records are exceptional. Code Noir records. Multiple fires (1788, 1794). French, Spanish, Creole, African, and immigrant heritage.

source_profiles.source_name: New Orleans Notarial Archives — Notarial Archives Research Center
source_profiles.repository: Notarial Archives Research Center, New Orleans
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Over 30 million pages of notarial acts from 1733 to present — unique to Louisiana civil law. Includes property sales, successions, marriage contracts, slave sales, emancipations, powers of attorney, and business records. The most comprehensive notarial collection in the Western Hemisphere.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some colonial-era acts damaged by fires of 1788 and 1794; most survive

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Ouachita Parish Clerk of Court (Monroe). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). Monroe-West Monroe is the major city of northeast Louisiana. University of Louisiana Monroe Library has regional history collections. Ouachita Parish Library has genealogy department. Cotton plantation parish in the antebellum period. Parent parish for numerous northeast Louisiana parishes. Pre-statehood French colonial records at Louisiana State Archives. Ouachita River was the main transportation route. Cross-reference with Morehouse, Richland, and Caldwell. Natural gas boom (1916+) brought industrial workers. Mixed French, Anglo-American, and African American heritage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Plaquemines Parish Clerk of Court (Belle Chasse). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). BURNED PARISH — Hurricane Katrina (2005) severely damaged courthouse and records. Mississippi River delta below New Orleans. Fort Jackson and Fort St. Philip (Civil War records at NARA). Citrus farming (pre-Katrina) and oil/gas industry. Catholic church registers at Our Lady of Good Harbor (Buras) and other parishes. Significant Croatian/Dalmatian and Filipino fishing communities. Plaquemines Parish Library rebuilding collections. French and Spanish colonial records at Louisiana State Archives. The parish extends to the mouth of the Mississippi.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; River Road plantation region; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Pointe Coupee Parish Clerk of Court (New Roads). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). One of the oldest settled areas in Louisiana — French post established 1720s. Pointe Coupee Slave Conspiracy (1795) is well-documented. Major sugar and cotton plantation parish with extensive enslaved persons records. Catholic church registers at St. Francis of Assisi (Pointe Coupee) from colonial era. Parlange Plantation area. False River (oxbow lake) communities. Pointe Coupee Parish Library has local history. French and Spanish colonial notarial records. Cross-reference with West Baton Rouge and Iberville for related families.

county_metadata.record_losses: Civil War destruction — Union Army burned Alexandria (1864), destroying courthouse and many records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Central Louisiana; significant free people of color community
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Rapides Parish Clerk of Court (Alexandria). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). BURNED PARISH — Union Army burned Alexandria in 1864 during the Red River Campaign, destroying the courthouse and many records. Central Louisiana hub. Pre-Civil War records largely lost — check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies. Louisiana State University at Alexandria has some regional collections. Kent House (colonial plantation museum) area. Catholic and Protestant records both important. Parent parish for Grant, Vernon, and parts of other parishes. Cross-reference with Natchitoches and Avoyelles for colonial-era records. Red River and Bayou Rapides families.

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Conveyance Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Rapides Parish Clerk of Court, Alexandria
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Conveyance (deed) books for Rapides Parish from 1807 forward; Louisiana uses civil law system — property transfers recorded as notarial acts
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction — Union Army burned Alexandria (1864), destroying courthouse and many records

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Succession Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Rapides Parish Clerk of Court, Alexandria
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Succession records (Louisiana's civil law equivalent of probate) for Rapides Parish; includes inventories, family meetings (conseil de famille), and partition records naming heirs
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction — Union Army burned Alexandria (1864), destroying courthouse and many records

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Marriage Records — Clerk of Court / Catholic Church Registers
source_profiles.repository: Rapides Parish Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil marriage licenses and Catholic church marriage registers for Rapides Parish; church records often predate civil registration
source_profiles.survival_notes: Statewide vital records registration from 1914; earlier records at parish level and in church registers

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Catholic Church Sacramental Registers
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of New Orleans / Diocese of Lafayette / Diocese of Baton Rouge / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic sacramental registers (baptism, marriage, burial) for Rapides Parish; includes separate registers for enslaved persons and free people of color in colonial and antebellum periods

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Enslaved Persons Records — Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives
source_profiles.repository: Rapides Parish Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Enslaved persons records for Rapides Parish including succession inventories listing enslaved persons by name, age, and value; Catholic church baptismal registers of enslaved persons; Code Noir records; bills of sale

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Free People of Color Records — Notarial Archives / Church Registers
source_profiles.repository: Rapides Parish Clerk of Court / Archdiocese of New Orleans / Louisiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of gens de couleur libres (free people of color) in Rapides Parish including separate sacramental registers, property records, militia records, and manumission acts under the Code Noir

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Native American and Tribal Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Louisiana State Archives / Tribal cultural centers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Multiple tribal removal-era records; central Louisiana crossroads; BIA records at NARA Record Group 75; colonial French and Spanish interactions with Native peoples

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Colonial Land Grants and US Land Patents
source_profiles.repository: Louisiana State Archives / BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: French and Spanish colonial land grants, US territorial confirmations, and General Land Office patents for Rapides Parish area; Louisiana State Land Office records

source_profiles.source_name: Rapides Parish Tax Assessment Rolls — Louisiana State Archives
source_profiles.repository: Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Parish tax assessment rolls for Rapides Parish listing property owners, real estate, and (before 1865) enslaved persons; useful when census records are missing
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil War destruction — Union Army burned Alexandria (1864), destroying courthouse and many records
